storyDialogue.text: ARES STATION — MARS ORBIT. SOL 1,847. CREW COMPLEMENT: 2 OF 6. LIFE SUPPORT: 31% NOMINAL.
LAST EARTH CONTACT: 142 DAYS AGO. SIGNAL STATUS: LOST.
Mission log, Sol 1847. Commander Elara Wells recording. We've completed another orbit of Mars with no response from Houston, ESA, or any ground station.
Engineer Orin Vasquez and I are the only crew remaining after the decompression event on Sol 1705. The others... I've logged their names. I won't say them again.
Oxygen recycler efficiency is dropping. We have approximately ninety days of breathable air. After that, the math gets very simple.
Commander. I've been running diagnostics on the long-range antenna array. I found something.
Good news or bad news?
Depends on your definition. The antenna isn't damaged. The hardware is fully functional. We're not receiving Earth's signal because Earth isn't transmitting.
That's impossible. Even if mission control went dark, there are civilian broadcasts, military channels, amateur radio operators. Earth is loud. Earth is always loud.
I swept every frequency from 3 kilohertz to 300 gigahertz. Every band is silent. Complete electromagnetic silence from the direction of Earth.
...How long have you known?
I confirmed it six hours ago. I spent the first four hoping I was wrong.
OPTICAL TELESCOPE — EARTH OBSERVATION WINDOW. ATMOSPHERIC ANALYSIS IN PROGRESS.
The telescope shows Earth's atmosphere has changed. The spectral analysis indicates massive particulate density in the upper atmosphere. Something obscured the surface.
Impact winter. Volcanic event. Nuclear exchange. Take your pick. Any of them could produce that kind of atmospheric signature.
Or all of them at once.
Commander, we need to talk about what this means for us. Practically.
I know what it means.
There is no rescue mission. There is no resupply. We are the last two human beings who are definitively alive, orbiting a planet we can't land on, breathing air that's slowly running out.
We can land on Mars.
The descent module was designed for four crew with full ground support. We have two crew and no support. The survival probability is—
Higher than zero. Which is what we have if we stay up here.
You want to land on Mars. With no habitat prepared. No supply cache. No way home.
I want to live, Orin. Even if it's only for a few more months. I want to stand on solid ground and look at the sky and know that I didn't give up.
...That's not rational.
No. It's human.
DESCENT MODULE PRE-FLIGHT CHECK. ALL SYSTEMS GREEN. ESTIMATED LANDING WINDOW: 7 HOURS.
I've packed everything we can carry. Water recycler, medical kit, seed bank from the botany experiments. If we land near the ice deposits at Korolev Crater, we might have water.
Might.
It's the best word I have.
Before we go... I want to send one last transmission. Even if no one hears it.
This is Commander Elara Wells, Ares Station, Sol 1847. To anyone receiving this message — on Earth, in orbit, anywhere in the solar system.
We don't know what happened to you. We saw the silence, and we chose not to accept it as an ending.
Engineer Vasquez and I are descending to the surface of Mars. We carry seeds, water, and the complete digital archive of human civilization.
If you find this transmission, know that we tried. That's all anyone can do.
TRANSMISSION SENT. NO ACKNOWLEDGMENT RECEIVED. DESCENT SEQUENCE INITIATED.
SIGNAL LOSS IN 3... 2... 1...
